ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for crispy honey-glazed chicken. FOODON_03302051 (fried chicken food product) is the closest parent. The honey glaze and Turkish preparation style are not captured by this term.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers cooked chicken meat generically. No specific code exists for crispy honey-glazed chicken in the FoodEx2 catalogue. The coating and glaze components are not represented.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for crispy honey chicken as a prepared dish. SNOMED CT does not provide granular prepared-dish codes at this level.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of crispy honey chicken is approximately 300 g, comprising several pieces of battered and fried chicken coated in honey glaze, served as a main course.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken, broilers or fryers, meat and skin, cooked, fried, batter (USDA SR Legacy, NDB No. 05062) adapted with honey glaze component from USDA FoodData Central honey entry (FDC ID 169640)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy and Foundation Foods)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for crispy honey chicken as a composite Turkish restaurant dish. Nutrient values are derived by adapting USDA SR Legacy NDB 05062 (batter-fried chicken, meat and skin, per 100 g: energy 289 kcal, protein 27.5 g, fat 16.9 g, saturated fat 4.5 g, carbohydrate 8.5 g, sugar 0.3 g, fibre 0.3 g, sodium 290 mg) and blending in a proportional honey-glaze contribution (USDA FDC ID 169640: honey, per 100 g: energy 304 kcal, carbohydrate 82.4 g, sugar 82.1 g) at an estimated 10–12% glaze weight fraction of the finished dish. Final blended values are rounded to one decimal place. This is an estimated composition; confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of honey and honey-containing foods; analogous estimate using battered fried chicken (GI ~45) and honey (GI ~55–58) weighted composite
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for crispy honey chicken as a composite dish. GI is estimated as a weighted composite: battered fried chicken (GI ~45, low-medium, University of Sydney GI Database reference for fried chicken with coating) combined with honey glaze (GI ~55–58, University of Sydney GI Database, honey entries). At approximately 10–12% glaze fraction by weight, the blended GI is estimated at 55 (categorized as low). Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (55 × 16.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 3) / 100 = 27.7 g. This is an analogous estimate; a direct measurement is recommended for clinical use.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~310 mg per 100 g; ~930 mg per 300 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: A single 300 g serving provides approximately 930 mg sodium, representing about 40% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and avoid additional high-sodium condiments such as soy sauce or salt.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Honey glaze contributes significant free sugars (~9.2 g sugar per 100 g) and a glycemic index of 55 (categorized as low GI) with a high glycemic load of 27.7 per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: The honey glaze substantially raises the free-sugar and glycemic load of this dish; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to 150 g or fewer, pair with non-starchy vegetables to blunt postprandial glucose response, and monitor blood glucose after consumption.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~3.8 g per 100 g; ~11.4 g per serving) and deep-frying oil absorption
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Deep-frying increases total and saturated fat content; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption to occasional servings, request reduced-oil preparation where possible, and balance the meal with fibre-rich vegetables to mitigate LDL-raising effects of saturated fat.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Crispy Honey Chicken is a popular poultry dish served at Turkish and Middle Eastern restaurants, including in Singapore where it appears on the menu of Sultan Palace Turkish and Lebanese Cuisine. The dish consists of chicken pieces — typically bone-in thighs, drumsticks, or boneless breast strips — that are coated in a seasoned batter of wheat flour, cornstarch, egg, garlic, salt, and black pepper, then deep-fried until golden and crunchy. After frying, the chicken is finished with a generous honey glaze, which caramelises slightly on the hot surface to create a sweet, sticky, and aromatic coating. The result is a dish with contrasting textures: a shatteringly crisp exterior and juicy, tender chicken meat inside, balanced by the floral sweetness of honey and a subtle savoury depth from soy sauce and garlic. Nutritionally, crispy honey chicken is energy-dense at approximately 265 kcal per 100 g, with a meaningful protein contribution of 22.5 g per 100 g supporting satiety and muscle maintenance. The honey glaze raises the free-sugar content to approximately 9.2 g per 100 g and elevates the glycemic index to 55 (categorized as low GI), making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. Sodium content of approximately 310 mg per 100 g warrants attention for those with hypertension. The dish is halal-certified in the context of Sultan Palace and is free from dairy but contains gluten (wheat flour) and egg allergens. It is typically served as a main course, often accompanied by rice, flatbread, or salad, and is priced at approximately SGD 25 per portion in Singapore.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for crispy honey chicken as a composite Turkish restaurant dish. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy batter-fried chicken (NDB 05062) with a proportional honey-glaze contribution from USDA FDC honey data (FDC ID 169640). GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database references for fried chicken and honey. Medium confidence reflects the composite estimation approach rather than a direct measurement of this specific dish.
notes: 1. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reliably reported for composite fried chicken dishes in the USDA SR Legacy source used; a direct measurement would be required for clinical reporting. 2. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the primary USDA SR Legacy source entry used for this adaptation. 3. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is set to 0.1 g per 100 g reflecting small residual trans fats from deep-frying oil; this is consistent with USDA SR Legacy data for batter-fried chicken. 4. GI is an analogous estimate (GI 55, categorized as low) derived from University of Sydney GI Database references for batter-fried chicken and honey; a direct GI measurement of this dish is recommended for clinical use. 5. The dish is served at Sultan Palace Turkish and Lebanese Cuisine, Boat Quay, Singapore, at SGD 25 per portion. 6. Halal status is inferred from the restaurant context (Turkish and Lebanese cuisine establishment in Singapore); formal halal certification should be verified with the restaurant. 7. Multilingual names are provided for Singaporean context (English, Chinese Simplified, Chinese Traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) as the dish is served in Singapore, supplemented by Turkish romanized alias (Bal Tavuk). 8. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy NDB 05062 and are consistent with the adapted composition.
